triggers.label: Establishment of the Roman imperial title under Augustus
triggers.description: From 27 BC onward Octavian, the adopted heir of Julius Caesar, carried Caesar as part of his name and ruled as the first Roman emperor. His successors adopted Caesar as a standing element of the imperial titulature, converting the family name into a title of office; Germanic peoples borrowed it early as Kaisar/Kaiser.

triggers.label: Demosthenes' Philippics against Philip II (from 351 BC)
triggers.description: From 351 BC the Athenian orator Demosthenes delivered a series of vehement speeches urging resistance to Philip II of Macedon; these were called the 'Philippics'. Cicero later borrowed the name for his attacks on Mark Antony. The proper name generalized to mean any bitter denunciatory oration.

triggers.label: Construction of the tomb of Mausolus at Halicarnassus
triggers.description: Around 353 to 351 BC a monumental tomb was built at Halicarnassus for Mausolus, satrap of Caria, and his sister-wife Artemisia. Its fame as one of the Seven Wonders made the ruler's name (Greek Mausoleion) the generic word for any grand tomb.

triggers.label: Plato founds his school in the grove Akademeia
triggers.description: Around 387 BC Plato established his philosophical school in the grove Akademeia outside Athens. The school took the name of the place, and the place name thereby came to denote an institution of learning, the meaning carried into Latin academia and the modern languages.

triggers.label: Draco's Athenian law code (c. 621 BC)
triggers.description: The Athenian lawgiver Draco issued the city's first written code around 621 BC, notorious for prescribing death for even minor offences. The proverbial severity of these laws gave English 'draconian', which generalized to mean any disproportionately harsh measure.

triggers.label: Crusader contact with the Nizari Ismailis
triggers.description: During the Crusades the Nizari Ismaili order of Syria and Persia (active from c. 1090) became known to European chroniclers under the Arabic pejorative hashishin. Their reputation for targeted killing of political and military figures attached the killing sense to the name; later legend, popularised by Marco Polo, reinforced it.

triggers.label: Albigensian Crusade against the Cathars
triggers.description: Pope Innocent III launched the Albigensian Crusade in 1209 to suppress the Cathar sect. The sustained inquisitorial campaign made the group name (Latin Cathari, German Katzer/Ketzer) the standard word for heretics in general.

triggers.label: Belief that the moon (Luna) caused recurring madness
triggers.description: Medieval medicine, following classical sources, held that fits of insanity recurred with the phases of the moon (Latin luna). This belief produced Late Latin lunaticus 'moon-struck', borrowed into English by the late 13th century; the sense later broadened to wild folly.

triggers.label: Venetian and Ragusan plague isolation regime
triggers.description: In 1377 the Great Council of Ragusa (Dubrovnik) imposed a thirty-day (trentina) isolation on arrivals from plague areas; Venice and other ports extended it to forty days (quarantina). The fixed forty-day ship isolation against the Black Death attached the disease-control meaning to the number-word.

triggers.label: Figured cloth from Damascus
triggers.description: The patterned figured silk traded from Damascus carried the city name. Through Italian Damasco it entered German as Damast from the late medieval period.

triggers.label: The Harpies of Greek myth
triggers.description: The Harpies of Greek myth, whose name means 'snatchers', were winged monsters that seized and fouled food. The figure entered English by the late 14th century, and the extended sense of a repulsively greedy or grasping person is recorded from about 1400.

triggers.label: Founding of the Venetian Lazzaretto Vecchio
triggers.description: In 1423 Venice converted the island of Santa Maria di Nazaret into a permanent plague hospital and quarantine station, the Lazzaretto Vecchio. The island name (blended with St Lazarus) generalised into a common noun for an isolation hospital and spread into German as Lazarett.

triggers.label: Astrological temperament of those born under Saturn
triggers.description: In medieval astrology Saturn was the most remote, coldest, and slowest of the known planets, and those born under it were held to be gloomy, grave, and sluggish. The planetary adjective (mid-15c.) carried this morose sense from the outset.

triggers.label: Corinth raisin trade
triggers.description: Small dried grapes were exported from Corinth as 'raisins of Corauntz'. The place name was clipped to 'currant', with the final -s reanalysed as a plural ending, and became the fruit name.

triggers.label: Protestant Reformation in the German lands
triggers.description: The Reformation beginning 1517 sharply increased anticlericalism. Criticism of worldly, pluralist, and corrupt clergy made 'Pfaffe' a vehicle for contempt, fixing the pejorative sense.

triggers.label: Luther's Bible translation 1522-1534
triggers.description: Luther's translation systematically used 'fromm' for the biblical concept of God-fearing righteousness, transposing the word into the religious sphere and displacing its earlier general sense of capability.

triggers.label: Protestant Reformation and the valorisation of secular vocation (Beruf)
triggers.description: Luther's concept of the Beruf (calling/vocation) and the Protestant ethic elevated secular labour to a morally dignified activity, helping transform Arbeit from mere toil to valued work.

triggers.label: Cloth of Calicut (Kozhikode)
triggers.description: Europeans first obtained the cotton cloth at the Malabar-coast port of Calicut (modern Kozhikode); the place name became the cloth name 'calico', attested in English from the 1530s.

triggers.label: Machiavelli's 'Il Principe' (1532)
triggers.description: Niccolo Machiavelli's treatise 'Il Principe', written 1513 and printed posthumously in 1532, advised rulers that the ends of holding power could justify deceit and cruelty. Its reception, often as a byword for amoral statecraft, made 'Machiavellian' a synonym for cunning and unscrupulous scheming in English from around 1600.

triggers.label: Rabelais's 'Gargantua' (1534)
triggers.description: Francois Rabelais's comic novel 'La Vie tres horrifique du grand Gargantua' (1534) centred on a giant of prodigious size and appetite. The character's fame made 'gargantuan' an English adjective for the enormous, attested from the 1590s.

triggers.label: Alcyone and the calm of the halcyon days
triggers.description: In Greek myth Alcyone, daughter of Aeolus, was changed into a kingfisher; the gods stilled the winter seas for fourteen halcyon days while she nested. The legend (transmitted through Ovid and the medieval bestiaries) fixed the bird-name as a figure for calm, fixing the adjective by the 1540s.

triggers.label: The Cretan Labyrinth of Daedalus and the Minotaur
triggers.description: In Greek myth Daedalus built the Labyrinth on Crete, a maze so intricate that the Minotaur could not escape it. The name, in English from about 1400 for the maze, yielded by the 1540s the figurative sense of any bewildering, intricate situation.

triggers.label: Student slang origin and 19th c. spread via the Backfischroman
triggers.description: DWDS records the figurative sense 'adolescent girl' as student slang from the mid-16th century. The genre of the 'Backfischroman' for young female readers later spread the figurative sense across the German-speaking public in the 19th century.

triggers.label: Confusion of guinea fowl with the American bird
triggers.description: Guinea fowl reached England through Turkish merchants and were called 'turkey'. When the larger New World bird arrived in the 16th century it was conflated with that fowl and inherited the country name.

triggers.label: Nicot introduces tobacco to the French court
triggers.description: In 1560 Jean Nicot, French ambassador to Portugal, sent tobacco plants and seeds to the court in Paris. The plant genus Nicotiana was named for him, and the alkaloid isolated in 1828 took the name nicotine.

triggers.label: Bleaching of a degree adverb into a downtoner
triggers.description: No single datable event. The adverbial 'pretty' weakened gradually from 'in a clever or fine manner' to a moderating downtoner over the 16th-17th centuries, a routine path for degree adverbs. The anchor year is the earliest adverbial attestation, not a cause.

triggers.label: Genoese cloth trade
triggers.description: Genoa (French Genes) was the European source of a sturdy fustian cloth. The fabric carried the city name into English as 'jean'; the trousers cut from it later took the same name.

triggers.label: Songbird of the Canary Islands
triggers.description: The finch native to the Canary Islands was called 'Canary-bird' (1570s) for its place of origin, then shortened to 'canary' (1650s). The islands' Latin name Insula Canaria ('island of dogs') is itself debated.

triggers.label: Ironic reversal of a term of praise
triggers.description: No single datable event. The positive 'distinguished' was applied ironically (egregiously bad), and the ironic, negative sense displaced the original within a few decades in the later 16th century. The anchor year is the earliest negative attestation, not a cause.

triggers.label: Astrological disposition of those born under Jupiter
triggers.description: Renaissance astrology held that those born under the sign of Jupiter (Jove), the most benign planet, were of a happy and generous disposition. The planetary adjective and the merry sense both surface in the 1580s, the cheerful meaning growing directly out of the astrological belief.

triggers.label: Reputation of Laconian (Spartan) terseness
triggers.description: Classical writers recorded the Laconians of Sparta as famously curt of speech. Renaissance humanists revived the adjective 'laconic' from the region name to mean terse expression.

triggers.label: The Sirens of Greek myth
triggers.description: In Greek myth the Sirens lured passing sailors to wreck with their song, as in the Odyssey. The mythical name, in English from the mid-14th century, yielded by the 1580s the figurative sense of a dangerously alluring woman or temptation.

triggers.label: Myth of Hercules and the Twelve Labours
triggers.description: Hercules (Greek Herakles) was the archetypal hero of superhuman strength, set the Twelve Labours as penance. His legend, transmitted through classical literature, made 'herculean' an adjective for any task of extraordinary difficulty or any feat of great power, attested in English around 1600.

triggers.label: Greek goddess Nemesis
triggers.description: Nemesis personified divine retribution in Greek religion, punishing arrogance and the disturbance of due proportion. Adopted into English as the name of the goddess, the word generalized to mean an instrument of inescapable downfall and then an unbeatable adversary.

triggers.label: Proteus, the shape-shifting sea-god
triggers.description: Proteus, the Greek sea-god of Homeric and later myth, could elude capture by changing into any shape. The figure lent his name to the adjective 'protean', recorded in the 1590s, for anything endlessly variable or versatile.

triggers.label: Greek myth of Tantalus
triggers.description: In Greek myth Tantalus was condemned in the underworld to stand in a pool beneath fruit-laden boughs, both of which drew away whenever he reached to drink or eat. The verb 'tantalize' was coined in late sixteenth-century English directly from his name to mean teasing with the unattainable.

triggers.label: Pejoration by negative collocation
triggers.description: No single datable event. 'Notorious' was repeatedly applied to bad or scandalous matters (notorious offenders, notorious crimes), and the negative collocations transferred their colour to the word over the late 16th century. The anchor year is a representative attestation, not a cause.

triggers.label: Mercator's 'Atlas' map collection (1595)
triggers.description: Gerardus Mercator titled his posthumously completed 1595 collection of maps 'Atlas', invoking the Titan who bore the heavens, and its frontispiece depicted the figure. The work's fame fixed 'atlas' as the common word for a bound book of maps, an English usage attested from 1636.

triggers.label: Hermes Trismegistus and the hermetic seal
triggers.description: Hermes Trismegistus, the legendary sage identified by alchemists with the god Hermes, was credited with a secret process for sealing a glass tube perfectly airtight. From this 'hermetic seal' the adjective acquired its everyday sense 'airtight', attested in the adverb from about 1600.

triggers.label: Greek myth of the god Pan and 'panic' fear
triggers.description: In Greek myth the god Pan inhabited wild and lonely places and was held to inspire a sudden, irrational, contagious terror, the 'panikon deima'. Through Greek and Latin the adjective passed into English as 'panic', and the standalone noun for sudden fright emerged by the early eighteenth century.

triggers.label: Wine of Xeres (Jerez de la Frontera)
triggers.description: The Spanish town of Xeres (modern Jerez de la Frontera) near Cadiz produced the fortified wine; its name entered English as 'sherris', from which 'sherry' was back-formed as a mistaken singular.

triggers.label: Stentor, the loud-voiced herald of Homer's Iliad
triggers.description: Stentor was the herald of the Greek forces at Troy, described by Homer as having a voice as loud as fifty men. The figure, known from the eighth-century BC epic, lent his name to the adjective for a powerful voice.

triggers.label: The Gunpowder Plot of Guy Fawkes
triggers.description: In 1605 Guy Fawkes was caught guarding explosives beneath the English Parliament in the Gunpowder Plot. The annual burning of his effigy ('a guy') on 5 November fixed his name to a grotesque figure, from which the broadened modern sense 'man, fellow' later developed.

triggers.label: Publication of Cervantes' Don Quixote
triggers.description: The first part of Miguel de Cervantes' novel Don Quixote was published in 1605. The hero's idealistic but deluded chivalry made his name a byword for romantic impracticality, yielding the adjective 'quixotic' through metaphor.

triggers.label: Fame of Spa's mineral springs
triggers.description: The Belgian town of Spa became the leading European destination for mineral-water cures. English visitors generalised the place name to mean any such spring resort, and later any health and bathing establishment.

triggers.label: Founding of the Congregatio de Propaganda Fide
triggers.description: In 1622 Pope Gregory XV established the Congregatio de Propaganda Fide to coordinate Catholic missionary activity during the Counter-Reformation. Etymonline records the secular sense 'movement to propagate an ideology' from 1790 and the political sense from the First World War, which was 'originally not pejorative'; the manipulative, negative sense settled in the 20th century. The Roman institution is the lexical origin.

triggers.label: Gradual weakening of a moral condemnation
triggers.description: No single datable event. The strong moral sense 'wicked' weakened over the 17th century to the milder 'mischievous, disobedient', a common softening of moral terms when applied colloquially and to children. The anchor year is a representative attestation, not a cause.

triggers.label: Belief in a Sardinian plant causing a fatal grin
triggers.description: Greek 'sardonios' (scornful laughter) was reshaped under the influence of 'Sardonios', Sardinian, from a belief that eating a plant called 'sardonion' (plant from Sardinia) produced facial convulsions resembling laughter, usually followed by death. The ancient etymology is disputed.

triggers.label: Astrological temperament of those born under Mercury
triggers.description: Renaissance astrology held that people born under the planet Mercury were quick, lively, and changeable. The planetary adjective (attested late 14c.) acquired the temperament sense by the 1640s, reinforced by association with the fluid, ever-moving qualities of quicksilver (also called mercury).

triggers.label: Reputation of Spartan austerity
triggers.description: Ancient accounts of Sparta's harsh military upbringing and plain living made the civic name a byword for austerity. The adjective generalised to mean severe simplicity and frugal endurance.

triggers.label: Pascal's work on hydrostatics and pressure
triggers.description: Blaise Pascal's experiments on hydrostatics, the barometer and the transmission of fluid pressure made his name a fitting honour for the unit of pressure, adopted in 1971 at the 14th General Conference on Weights and Measures.

triggers.label: Croat cavalry in the Thirty Years War
triggers.description: Croat (Crabat) light cavalry fighting for the Catholic League wore linen neck scarves. The fashion was imitated in France from the 1650s, and the ethnonym 'cravate' became the name of the garment.

triggers.label: Baroque court culture and the Kavalierstour, 17th century
triggers.description: Baroque court society and the educational grand tour of young nobles (the Kavalierstour) made the 'Kavalier' the model of polished courtly manners. The word broadened from a mounted noble to any man of courteous, chivalrous bearing.

triggers.label: Cassandra, the unbelieved prophetess of Troy
triggers.description: In Greek myth Apollo gave Cassandra the gift of prophecy, then cursed it so that, though she spoke truth, none would believe her; her warnings about the fall of Troy were ignored. The name has been used figuratively since the 1660s for an unheeded prophet of doom.

triggers.label: Wine production in the Champagne province of France
triggers.description: The former province of Champagne in northeast France gave its name to the wine made there; the term then narrowed to the sparkling wine produced by methode champenoise.

triggers.label: Marine insurance and salvage law
triggers.description: The body of maritime salvage law and the marine-insurance market (notably Lloyd's of London) made 'salvage' a common commercial term for recovering property from peril. From this base the sense broadened in the 19th century to reclaiming usable material generally, and later to figurative rescue.

triggers.label: German university student slang, late 17th century
triggers.description: In German university towns students adopted 'Philister' as a label for townsmen and non-students, opposing themselves to the Biblical enemies of the chosen people. A 1693 Jena funeral sermon for a student killed by townsmen is a frequently cited early attestation. The sense broadened to the narrow-minded, unintellectual person.

triggers.label: Textile production at Nimes
triggers.description: The serge cloth exported as 'serge de Nimes' carried the city name. English speakers clipped the phrase to 'de Nim' and reanalysed it as the fabric name 'denim'.

triggers.label: Fenelon's 'Les Aventures de Telemaque' (1699)
triggers.description: Mentor is a minor figure in Homer's Odyssey, but Francois Fenelon's enormously popular didactic romance 'Les Aventures de Telemaque' (1699) made Mentor the central wise counsellor to the young prince. The book's pan-European success turned the proper name into a common noun for a trusted adviser, attested in English from the mid eighteenth century.

triggers.label: Soziale Generalisierung eines Standestitels
triggers.description: Kein einzelnes datierbares Ereignis. 'Frau' verlor ueber Jahrhunderte den exklusiven Adelsbezug und wurde zur allgemeinen Bezeichnung jeder erwachsenen Frau, waehrend 'Weib' absank. Das Ankerjahr markiert die etablierte allgemeine Bedeutung, keine Ursache.

triggers.label: Plumier names the genus Fuchsia for Leonhart Fuchs
triggers.description: The French botanist Charles Plumier named the genus Fuchsia in 1703 in honour of the German botanist Leonhart Fuchs. The English word is attested by 1753, and the colour sense by 1921.

triggers.label: Production of eau de Cologne
triggers.description: From 1709 a scented water marketed as 'eau de Cologne' was produced in the city. Its fame fixed the city name as the English word for a light perfume.

triggers.label: Fahrenheit invents the mercury thermometer and scale
triggers.description: Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit invented the mercury-in-glass thermometer and proposed his temperature scale in 1714. The scale came to be named after him, first attested as the word 'fahrenheit' in 1753.

triggers.label: the South Sea Bubble financial crisis (1720)
triggers.description: The 1720 collapse of the South Sea Company's inflated share price, named the 'South Sea Bubble', fixed 'bubble' as the standard metaphor for a speculative market boom that bursts.

triggers.label: Swift's 'Gulliver's Travels' (1726)
triggers.description: In Jonathan Swift's satire 'Gulliver's Travels' (1726) the Yahoos are vicious, filthy creatures of human form, contrasted with the rational Houyhnhnms. Swift coined the name; it passed into general English as a term of contempt for a brutish or loutish person.

triggers.label: Celsius proposes the centigrade temperature scale
triggers.description: Anders Celsius proposed a centigrade temperature scale in 1742. The unit was formally named 'degree Celsius' in 1948 by the 9th General Conference on Weights and Measures, to honour him and avoid confusion with the centigrade angular measure.

triggers.label: Angora (Ancyra / Ankara), Turkey
triggers.description: The long-haired goat and its wool took the name of Angora, the old form of Ancyra (modern Ankara); 'Angora goat' is attested 1745 and the wool sense by 1810, the city name standing for the material.

triggers.label: Tenure of Etienne de Silhouette as controller-general of finances
triggers.description: Etienne de Silhouette served briefly as French controller-general of finances in 1759. His name became a byword for cheapness and austerity, and was attached to the inexpensive shadow-outline portraits then in fashion, from which the general sense of an outline shape developed.

triggers.label: John Montagu, 4th Earl of Sandwich, popularises bread-and-filling meals
triggers.description: Around 1762 John Montagu, 4th Earl of Sandwich, was reported to order meat served between bread so he could eat without leaving the gaming table. His title became attached to the food, producing the modern food sense via metonymy.

triggers.label: Premiere of Sheridan's The Rivals
triggers.description: Richard Brinsley Sheridan's comedy The Rivals premiered in 1775. Its character Mrs Malaprop habitually confused similar-sounding words; her name (from French 'mal a propos') became the term for such verbal blunders by metaphor.

triggers.label: Watt's improved steam engine
triggers.description: From 1776 James Watt's separate-condenser steam engine entered commercial use, transforming industry. His name was adopted for the unit of power by the British Association in 1882.

triggers.label: Franz Mesmer's animal magnetism in vogue in Paris
triggers.description: In the 1780s the physician Franz Mesmer popularised 'animal magnetism' as a healing practice in Paris. The verb 'mesmerize' was formed from his name to mean 'to hypnotise', then broadened by metaphor to 'captivate, fascinate'.

triggers.label: Shrapnel develops the bursting case shot
triggers.description: From 1784 Henry Shrapnel developed a shell filled with shot that burst in flight; the British Army adopted it in 1803 and it carried his name. By the 1940s (the Blitz) the word had broadened to mean shell fragments in general (a use lexicographers note as strictly erroneous).

triggers.label: Cavanilles names the genus Dahlia for Anders Dahl
triggers.description: The Spanish botanist Antonio Jose Cavanilles named the genus Dahlia in 1791 in honour of the Swedish botanist Anders Dahl. The English word is attested by 1804.

triggers.label: Galvani's bioelectricity experiments
triggers.description: Around 1791 Luigi Galvani published experiments on the twitching of frog-leg muscles under electric stimulation. The German verb formed from his name first meant to treat with current, then broadened to electroplating and figurative rousing.

triggers.label: Galvani's bioelectricity experiments
triggers.description: Around 1791 Luigi Galvani published experiments showing that frog-leg muscles twitch when touched by metal, which he attributed to 'animal electricity'. The verb derived from his name first meant to stimulate by current, then broadened to mean rousing into action.

triggers.label: The novels of the Marquis de Sade
triggers.description: The Marquis de Sade became notorious for the cruel sexual practices described in his novels. His name was taken into French as 'sadisme' and into English by 1888 as the term for pleasure in cruelty.

triggers.label: Proposal and adoption of the beheading machine in the French Revolution
triggers.description: In 1789 deputy Joseph-Ignace Guillotin proposed that capital punishment use a uniform mechanical device. The machine was adopted in 1792 during the French Revolution and was named after him by metonymy, despite his opposition to the eponym.

triggers.label: Henri Gregoire coins 'vandalisme' during the French Revolution
triggers.description: In 1794 the abbe Henri Gregoire coined the French term 'vandalisme' to denounce the revolutionary destruction of monuments and artworks, invoking the Vandals' reputation for sacking Rome. The word spread into German as 'Vandalismus' with its negative sense.

triggers.label: Neo-humanist cult of Bildung, late 18th to 19th century
triggers.description: The neo-humanist revival of Greek learning and the 19th c. ideal of Bildung made the Greek 'banausos' a learned term of contempt. In German educated usage 'Banause' narrowed to a person without taste or appreciation for art and the intellect.

triggers.label: German Romanticism and 19th c. nationalism
triggers.description: From around 1800 the Romantic movement and the subsequent national awakening invested 'Heimat' with sentimental and ideological meaning, turning a legal-territorial term into a concept of emotional belonging and origin.

triggers.label: Semantic bleaching of an assertive into an intensifier
triggers.description: There is no single datable event. The intensifier use arose gradually in literary English from the 18th century onward, as a strong assertion of truth weakened into a generic emphasiser. The anchor year is a representative attestation point, not a cause.

triggers.label: Volta invents the voltaic pile
triggers.description: In 1800 Alessandro Volta announced the voltaic pile, the first continuous source of electric current. His name was later attached to the unit of electric potential, adopted internationally in the 1880s.

triggers.label: The Dalmatia region
triggers.description: The spotted carriage dog was called 'Dalmatian dog' (1810), presumably for Dalmatia, and shortened to 'dalmatian' by 1893. Whether the breed truly originates in the region is debated among breeders.

triggers.label: Luddite machine-breaking uprisings
triggers.description: Between 1811 and 1816 textile workers in the English Midlands and north destroyed mechanized looms and frames, fearing loss of livelihood. The movement, invoking the legendary Ned Ludd, gave its name first to the participants and later, by broadening, to any opponent of technology.

triggers.label: Massachusetts redistricting under Governor Gerry
triggers.description: In 1812 Massachusetts redrew its senatorial districts to favour Governor Elbridge Gerry's party. A press cartoon likened one contorted district to a salamander, and the blend 'Gerry-mander' entered the language as a generic term for partisan map manipulation.

triggers.label: Thomas Bowdler's 'Family Shakespeare' (1818)
triggers.description: In 1818 Thomas Bowdler published an expurgated 'Family Shakespeare' with passages deemed indecent removed. His name became a verb (attested 1836) for the practice of purging text of supposedly offensive content.

triggers.label: Mary Shelley's 'Frankenstein' (1818)
triggers.description: Mary Shelley's novel 'Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus' (1818) told of a scientist whose animated creature turns against him. The name generalized to mean a creation that overpowers and destroys its creator, and (by popular confusion) the creature itself, attested figuratively from the 1830s.

triggers.label: Ampere founds electrodynamics
triggers.description: From 1820 Andre-Marie Ampere formulated the laws relating electric current and magnetism, founding electrodynamics. His name was adopted for the unit of current at the 1881 International Electrical Congress.

triggers.label: Procrustes, the bed-stretcher of Greek myth
triggers.description: Procrustes, the mythical robber of Attica, forced victims to fit his bed by stretching or maiming them. The figure (known from classical sources and revived in English use of the 1580s, with the adjective recorded from 1822) became a byword for enforced, arbitrary conformity.

triggers.label: Handling code at Rugby School
triggers.description: The distinctive handling game of football was associated with Rugby School from the 1820s. As football codes diverged, the school name was adopted for the handling code and its game.

triggers.label: Braille devises the raised-dot system
triggers.description: In 1824 the fifteen-year-old Louis Braille devised a six-dot tactile code for reading and writing, published in 1829. The system spread internationally and took his name.

triggers.label: Ohm publishes Ohm's law
triggers.description: In 1827 Georg Simon Ohm published 'Die galvanische Kette, mathematisch bearbeitet', stating the proportionality of current and voltage. His name became the unit of resistance, developed through the British Association unit work of the 1860s and internationally established in 1881 (per DWDS).

triggers.label: Ohm publishes Ohm's law
triggers.description: In 1827 Georg Simon Ohm published 'Die galvanische Kette, mathematisch bearbeitet', stating the proportionality of current and voltage. His name was adopted for the unit of resistance by the British Association in the 1860s.

triggers.label: Cogniard brothers' play 'La cocarde tricolore' (1831)
triggers.description: The 1831 vaudeville 'La cocarde tricolore' by the Cogniard brothers popularized the figure of Chauvin as the archetype of bellicose, boastful patriotism. From this the French 'chauvinisme' was coined (1843) and Latinized into German as 'Chauvinismus'.

triggers.label: Shawl production at Paisley, Scotland
triggers.description: The Scottish town of Paisley mass produced shawls with the curved teardrop motif; the place name became the name of the cloth and then the pattern, attested in English from 1834.

triggers.label: Morse and Vail develop the telegraph code
triggers.description: From 1838 Samuel Morse and Alfred Vail developed the signalling code for the electric telegraph, publicly demonstrated in 1838 and used on the first US telegraph line in 1844. The code became known by Morse's name.

triggers.label: Bourbon County, Kentucky
triggers.description: American corn whiskey was first made in Bourbon County, Kentucky (organized 1785); the county name attached to the spirit, attested by 1840.

triggers.label: 1840s socialist movement and the Communist Manifesto (1848)
triggers.description: In the 1840s the early socialist movement, and the Communist Manifesto of 1848, revived 'Proletariat' for the modern industrial working class and recast it from a Roman class of paupers into a positively charged collective political subject.

triggers.label: Student slang and 19th c. critique of the bourgeoisie
triggers.description: From the 18th c. students used 'Spiessbuerger' to mock the settled townsman as the opposite of the free academic. The 19th c. intellectual and artistic critique of bourgeois conformism fixed the short form 'Spiesser' as a pejorative for the narrow, philistine conformist.

triggers.label: Joule's work on the mechanical equivalent of heat
triggers.description: James Prescott Joule established the mechanical equivalent of heat in the 1840s, a foundation of the conservation of energy. His name was coined as the unit of electrical energy in 1882.

triggers.label: Sax patents the saxophone
triggers.description: In 1846 Adolphe Sax obtained a French patent for the family of instruments he had designed. The instruments and the word formed from his name spread rapidly through military and later jazz ensembles.

triggers.label: Thomson devises the absolute temperature scale
triggers.description: William Thomson proposed an absolute thermodynamic temperature scale in 1848. After he was ennobled as Baron Kelvin, the unit increment of thermodynamic temperature was renamed 'kelvin' in 1967 at the 13th General Conference on Weights and Measures.

triggers.label: 1848 socialist movement and Marx's Das Kapital (1867)
triggers.description: The Communist Manifesto of 1848 and Marx's Das Kapital (1867) gave 'Klasse' a precise socio-economic meaning, the social class defined by its relation to the means of production. The socialist labour movement spread this technical sense in German.

triggers.label: Pejoration ueber die Preisbedeutung
triggers.description: Kein einzelnes datierbares Ereignis. Ueber die Zwischenbedeutung 'preiswert' kippte 'billig' zur negativen Wertung 'minderwertig', als niedriger Preis mit geringer Qualitaet gleichgesetzt wurde. Das Ankerjahr ist eine repraesentative Belegstelle, keine Ursache.

triggers.label: gradual 19th-century generalisation of military 'Etappe'
triggers.description: DWDS records the military supply-zone sense from around 1700 and a generalised 'Tagesmarsch, Streckenabschnitt' (day's march, section of route) and figurative 'Zeit-, Entwicklungsabschnitt' (stage, phase) developing through the 19th century. No single datable event is recorded; the cause is gradual generalisation of the military term into everyday usage.

triggers.label: The Earl of Cardigan at the Charge of the Light Brigade
triggers.description: The 7th Earl of Cardigan reportedly wore a knitted woollen jacket while leading the Charge of the Light Brigade at Balaclava in 1854 during the Crimean War. The garment took his name, attested as 'cardigan' by 1868.

triggers.label: Jules Leotard performs the flying trapeze in a close-fitting garment
triggers.description: Jules Leotard, the popular French trapeze artist, performed in a close-fitting one-piece garment. After his death the garment was named for him, attested as 'leotards' by 1881.

triggers.label: Battle of Magenta (1859)
triggers.description: A brilliant crimson aniline dye introduced in 1860 was named in honour of the Battle of Magenta (1859), where French and Sardinian forces defeated the Austrians; the dye and colour took the battle's name.

triggers.label: Walton markets Linoleum floor covering
triggers.description: Frederick Walton coined and registered Linoleum around 1864 for his linseed-oil floor covering. Because he did not police the term, it was held to have become generic in an 1878 English court ruling, one of the earliest recorded cases of trademark genericide.

triggers.label: Pasteur develops controlled heat treatment of liquids
triggers.description: Around 1864 Louis Pasteur showed that gentle heating of wine and beer killed spoilage microbes without ruining the product. The process and the verb derived from his name spread once the germ theory of disease was accepted.

triggers.label: German labour movement and trade unions, 1860s-1870s
triggers.description: From the 1860s the rising German labour movement and the founding of trade unions fixed the English loan 'Streik' as the technical term for an organised collective stoppage of work to enforce demands.

triggers.label: Samuel Maverick's unbranded Texas cattle
triggers.description: Texas landowner Samuel A. Maverick was known for not branding his cattle, so unmarked strays came to be called 'mavericks' by the mid-1860s. The cattle sense broadened to mean any unattached, independent individual.

triggers.label: Sappho of Lesbos and her reception
triggers.description: The poet Sappho of Lesbos wrote of love between women. Nineteenth-century reception of her verse turned the island adjective 'Lesbian' into a term for female same-sex desire.

triggers.label: G. W. Hunt's 1878 music-hall song during the Russo-Turkish crisis
triggers.description: During the 1877-1878 crisis over British intervention against Russia, G. W. Hunt's music-hall song popularized by G. H. MacDermott carried the refrain 'we don't want to fight but by jingo if we do'. Supporters were dubbed 'jingoes' and 'jingoism' became a contemptuous label for belligerent patriotism.

triggers.label: Irish Land League ostracism of Captain Boycott
triggers.description: In 1880 the Irish Land League organized a campaign of social and economic isolation against land agent Charles Boycott after he refused to lower rents. The action drew international press coverage and his surname became the common term for the tactic.

triggers.label: Hamburg steak named for the city of Hamburg
triggers.description: The chopped-beef 'Hamburg steak' (attested by 1880) carried the name of the German port of Hamburg; 'hamburger' then narrowed to the sandwich (by 1909). The reason the city is invoked is not firmly established in the sources.

triggers.label: Sacher-Masoch's novels and Krafft-Ebing's coinage
triggers.description: Leopold von Sacher-Masoch featured submission and pain in works such as 'Venus in Furs' (1869). The German neurologist Richard von Krafft-Ebing coined 'Masochismus' from his name in 1883; the English 'masochism' is attested by 1892.

triggers.label: Gloves of Sweden (gants de Suede)
triggers.description: The French phrase 'gants de Suede', gloves of Sweden, named the soft napped leather; English took 'suede' from it (the phrase in English by 1859, the noun by 1884), the country name standing for the material.

triggers.label: Granola registered as a cereal brand
triggers.description: The name Granola was registered as a breakfast-cereal trademark around 1886 (a respelling of the earlier Granula). After the trademark lapsed the word passed into general use for any oat-based baked cereal.

triggers.label: Dinner jacket worn at Tuxedo Park
triggers.description: The semiformal evening jacket was first worn at the Tuxedo Park country club around 1886. The resort name attached to the garment and entered general use by 1889.

triggers.label: Mach studies supersonic shock waves
triggers.description: Around 1887 Ernst Mach photographed shock waves around projectiles moving faster than sound and described the relationship now expressed by the Mach number. The dimensionless number was named for him by Jakob Ackeret in 1929.

triggers.label: Hertz demonstrates electromagnetic waves
triggers.description: In 1888 Heinrich Hertz experimentally produced and detected electromagnetic (radio) waves, confirming Maxwell's theory. His name was adopted for the unit of frequency, formally ratified by the IEC in 1935 and the General Conference on Weights and Measures in 1960.

triggers.label: Hyperbolic bleaching of a fear word into praise
triggers.description: No single datable event. Like 'awful', 'tremendous' and 'dreadful', the word weakened through colloquial hyperbole from 'frightful' to 'very great' to 'excellent' over the 19th century. The anchor year marks the colloquial positive attestation, not a cause.

triggers.label: Tesla's work on electromagnetism and alternating current
triggers.description: Nikola Tesla's pioneering work on alternating current and electromagnetic devices made his name a fitting honour for the unit of magnetic flux density, adopted by 1960 at the 11th General Conference on Weights and Measures.

triggers.label: Homer's epic poem the Odyssey
triggers.description: Homer's Odyssey, the eighth-century BC epic of Odysseus's long voyage home from Troy, gave its title to English by 1600. The fame of that protracted, episodic journey produced the figurative common noun 'odyssey' for any long wandering, recorded from 1889.

triggers.label: Rudolf Diesel's engine patent
triggers.description: Rudolf Diesel patented his compression-ignition engine (German patent filed 1892, granted 1893) and published the design in 1893. The engine and, later, its fuel were named after the inventor, turning his surname into a generic technical term.

triggers.label: Sausage of Frankfurt am Main
triggers.description: A smoked sausage associated with Frankfurt am Main; German 'Frankfurter Wurst' was adopted in American English as 'frankfurter' (attested 1894), the city name standing for the product.

triggers.label: Roentgen's discovery of X-rays at Wuerzburg
triggers.description: On 8 November 1895 Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen discovered a new kind of radiation at the University of Wuerzburg. In German the rays and the resulting imaging were named after him (Roentgenstrahlen, roentgen), turning the surname into a generic medical and technical term.

triggers.label: Revaluation of worldliness from corruption to refinement
triggers.description: No single datable event. The loss of naive purity, once a defect (adulteration), was gradually reinterpreted as the positive quality of worldly refinement; the favourable 'worldly-wise' sense of persons is attested by 1895 (Etymonline). The anchor year marks the established positive sense, not a cause.

triggers.label: Becquerel's discovery of radioactivity
triggers.description: Henri Becquerel discovered natural radioactivity in 1896, sharing the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with the Curies. His name was adopted for the SI unit of radioactivity in 1975 at the 15th General Conference on Weights and Measures.

triggers.label: Marathon race at the 1896 Athens Olympics
triggers.description: The first modern Olympics of 1896 staged a long-distance race commemorating the legendary run from the plain of Marathon to Athens after the 490 BC battle. The event fixed the ancient place name as the name of the race.

triggers.label: The Curies' discovery of radium
triggers.description: Pierre and Marie Curie discovered radium and polonium in 1898 and pioneered the study of radioactivity. The unit of radioactivity was named in honour of Pierre Curie, first attested 1910.

triggers.label: London press police-court reports of summer 1898
triggers.description: In the summer of 1898 London newspaper police-court reports popularized 'Hooligan', probably from the comic-Irish surname Houlihan, as the label for unruly street youths. The proper-name origin generalized rapidly into a common noun for any violent young troublemaker.

triggers.label: Bayer launches Aspirin brand
triggers.description: In 1899 Bayer registered and marketed acetylsalicylic acid under the brand Aspirin. In several countries the trademark lapsed (in the United States it was ruled generic in 1921), and the brand name passed into common use as the name of the substance itself.

triggers.label: Coinage of 'Narcissism' as a clinical term
triggers.description: Havelock Ellis in 1898 compared a form of self-absorbed sexuality to the myth of Narcissus; in 1899 the German psychiatrist Paul Naecke coined 'Narzissmus' in 'Die sexuellen Perversitaeten'. The term entered English by 1905 and Freud's later work fixed its clinical sense, turning the mythic figure into a psychological category.

triggers.label: Otis markets the Escalator brand
triggers.description: The Otis Elevator Company introduced and trademarked the moving stairway under the brand Escalator around 1900. Widespread use of the brand as the ordinary name for moving stairways led a 1950 trademark ruling to find the word generic.

triggers.label: First flight of the airship LZ 1 over Lake Constance
triggers.description: On 2 July 1900 the first Zeppelin rigid airship, LZ 1, made its maiden flight near Friedrichshafen. The inventor's name became the everyday term for the airship type.

triggers.label: Thermos brand vacuum flask launched
triggers.description: In 1904 the brand Thermos was adopted for the commercialised vacuum flask (based on James Dewar's earlier invention). The brand spread so widely that the word came to mean any vacuum flask, and a US court ruled it generic in 1963.

triggers.label: Baekeland markets Bakelite resin
triggers.description: In 1909 Leo Baekeland announced and branded Bakelite, the first fully synthetic plastic. The brand became widely used as a loose generic label for hard moulded phenolic plastics of the era.

triggers.label: Sanitas registers the Foen hair-dryer brand
triggers.description: The company Sanitas registered the trademark Foen for its electric hair dryer in 1909 (first model 1908). After AEG acquired Sanitas the brand persisted, and the word became the ordinary German term for any hair dryer.

triggers.label: Beiersdorf introduces Labello
triggers.description: Beiersdorf introduced the Labello lip-care stick in 1909. The brand became so dominant in German-speaking markets that the name is colloquially used for any lip balm.

triggers.label: French syndicalist promotion of 'sabotage' as industrial action
triggers.description: Around 1910 the French syndicalist movement (CGT) openly advocated 'sabotage' as a tactic, popularising the word; English borrowed it in this narrow labour sense before broadening it during and after the First World War.

triggers.label: Cellophane brand film commercialised
triggers.description: Jacques Brandenberger patented Cellophane and began commercial production of the transparent cellulose film around 1912 (perfected from his 1908 work). The brand became the ordinary word for the film; Wiktionary lists it among genericized trademarks.

triggers.label: First World War static trench warfare 1914-1918
triggers.description: The static line warfare of the First World War (1914-1918) fixed 'die Front' as the term for the combat zone in everyday German. From this war usage the word broadened to any line of confrontation, political or meteorological.

triggers.label: First World War trench warfare on the Western Front 1914-1918
triggers.description: After the front stalemated in late 1914, the war became a war of trenches. 'Schuetzengraben' became the emblematic word of the First World War (1914-1918) and later a figure for entrenched, irreconcilable positions.

triggers.label: British 'tank' codename and combat debut on the Somme
triggers.description: In 1915-1916 the British used 'tank' as a secrecy codename, disguising the new armoured vehicle as a water carrier. Its combat debut at the Somme in September 1916 fixed the military sense in public language.

triggers.label: First World War material battles, Verdun and the Somme 1916
triggers.description: The great material battles of 1916 (Verdun, the Somme) made sustained barrage fire a defining experience of the First World War. The artillery term 'Trommelfeuer' passed into figurative use for any relentless verbal barrage.

triggers.label: Coining of 'robot' in Karel Capek's play R.U.R.
triggers.description: Karel Capek's 1920 play R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots) introduced 'robot', from Czech 'robota' (forced labour). The neologism rapidly spread and broadened to mechanical and automated devices.

triggers.label: Rise of Mussolini's Fasci and the March on Rome
triggers.description: Mussolini founded the Fasci Italiani di Combattimento in 1919; the 1922 March on Rome brought the movement to power. English borrowed 'fascism' as its proper name, then broadened it to far-right authoritarianism generally.

triggers.label: B.F. Goodrich markets the Zipper boot
triggers.description: In 1923 the B.F. Goodrich Company adopted the name Zipper for rubber boots fitted with a hookless slide fastener. The catchy brand name was quickly transferred from the boots to the fastener, becoming the generic English word for the device.

triggers.label: Kimberly-Clark launches Kleenex
triggers.description: Kleenex disposable facial tissue was introduced in 1924 (Cellucotton Products Co.; the Kleenex trademark is now held by Kimberly-Clark). The brand became so dominant that the name began to be used for any paper tissue, a usage the company has since fought to limit.

triggers.label: Posthumous publication of Kafka's novels ('Der Prozess', 1925)
triggers.description: Franz Kafka's novels, including 'Der Prozess' (The Trial), published posthumously from 1925, depicted individuals trapped in opaque, irrational bureaucratic systems. As their reputation spread in the 1930s and 1940s, 'Kafkaesque' came to mean oppressively absurd, bureaucratic, and disorienting, beyond mere reference to the author.

triggers.label: Tempo paper handkerchief brand registered
triggers.description: In 1929 the Vereinigte Papierwerke registered the Tempo brand of disposable paper handkerchief at the Reichspatentamt in Berlin. The brand became the colloquial German word for any paper tissue.

triggers.label: Fischer markets Uhu adhesive
triggers.description: In 1932 the pharmacist August Fischer of Buehl presented the first ready-to-use clear synthetic-resin adhesive under the brand Uhu (named after the eagle-owl). The brand became a widely used colloquial label for clear tube glue.

triggers.label: Laszlo Biro patents the ballpoint pen
triggers.description: Laszlo Biro patented his ballpoint pen in 1938, and pens were marketed under the Biro name. In Britain and the Commonwealth the brand name became the ordinary word for any ballpoint pen.

triggers.label: DuPont announces nylon
triggers.description: In 1938 DuPont publicly announced nylon, a coined name for its new synthetic polyamide fibre, deliberately left as a generic descriptor rather than a registered trademark. It rapidly became the ordinary word for the polymer and the products made from it.

triggers.label: The Blitz: German bombing campaign against Britain
triggers.description: From September 1940 the German aerial bombing of Britain was named 'the Blitz' in English, clipped from 'Blitzkrieg'. The term then broadened to any sudden, intensive concentrated effort.

triggers.label: German invasion of Norway and Quisling's coup attempt
triggers.description: On 9 April 1940 Germany invaded Norway; Vidkun Quisling proclaimed himself head of government. Within weeks Allied press, notably a 1940 leader in The Times, turned his name into a generic term for traitor.

triggers.label: Japanese kamikaze suicide attacks in the Pacific War
triggers.description: Beginning at the Battle of Leyte Gulf in October 1944, organised Japanese suicide air attacks brought 'kamikaze' into English; the term later broadened to a figurative adjective for reckless self-destruction.

triggers.label: Operation Crossroads nuclear tests at Bikini Atoll
triggers.description: The United States conducted nuclear tests at Bikini Atoll in July 1946. Designer Louis Reard named his new swimsuit 'bikini' the same month, trading on the explosive notoriety of the place name.

triggers.label: coining of 'bit' (binary digit) and the rise of digital computing
triggers.description: The term 'bit', a contraction of 'binary digit' attributed to John W. Tukey, appeared in print in Claude Shannon's 1948 paper 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication'. The subsequent spread of digital computing established the computing sense, broadening the word from 'a small piece' into a technical unit of information.

triggers.label: Publication of Orwell's 'Nineteen Eighty-Four' (1949)
triggers.description: George Orwell's novel 'Nineteen Eighty-Four', published in 1949, portrayed a surveillance state of perpetual war, thought control, and falsified history. Its immediate impact, deepened by Orwell's death in 1950, turned 'Orwellian' from a mere authorial adjective into a byword for oppressive state manipulation.

triggers.label: gradual polysemous extension of 'attack' across the 20th century
triggers.description: No single datable event. Across the 20th century 'attack' extended from military/physical assault into medical, sporting and figurative domains. The cause is gradual polysemy and is undetermined; recorded here as a diffuse trigger so the change can be aligned with the SemEval signal without asserting a specific cause.

triggers.label: gradual polysemous extension of 'ball' across the 20th century
triggers.description: No single datable event. The distribution of 'ball' across its object, social-dance and figurative senses shifted gradually; the cause is undetermined polysemy. Recorded as a diffuse trigger so the change can be aligned with the SemEval signal without asserting a specific cause.

triggers.label: gradual polysemous extension of 'circle' across the 20th century
triggers.description: No single datable event. The social-group sense of 'circle' gained relative weight gradually; the cause is undetermined polysemy. Recorded as a diffuse trigger so the change can be aligned with the SemEval signal without asserting a specific cause.

triggers.label: gradual polysemous extension of 'edge' across the 20th century
triggers.description: No single datable event. The figurative senses of 'edge' (advantage, tension, frontier of innovation) gained relative weight gradually; the cause is undetermined polysemy. Recorded as a diffuse trigger so the change can be aligned with the SemEval signal without asserting a specific cause.

triggers.label: gradual polysemous extension of 'face' across the 20th century
triggers.description: No single datable event. The figurative and surface senses of 'face' gained relative weight gradually; the cause is undetermined polysemy. Recorded as a diffuse trigger so the change can be aligned with the SemEval signal without asserting a specific cause.

triggers.label: Xerox launches the 914 copier
triggers.description: In 1959 Haloid Xerox launched the Xerox 914, the first successful plain-paper office copier. The brand became so identified with photocopying that 'xerox' entered general use as a verb and noun, a usage the company actively discouraged.

triggers.label: Post-war West German youth culture and rock music
triggers.description: The emergence of rock and roll in West Germany in the late 1950s/early 1960s led young people to resemanticize 'toll' (the wild-ones/tolle quality) from a negative to an enthusiastically positive colloquial marker.

triggers.label: Umgangssprachliche Aufwertung eines Negativworts zum Ausruf
triggers.description: Kein einzelnes datierbares Ereignis. 'Wahnsinn' verblasste umgangssprachlich von 'Irrsinn' zum positiven Ausruf der Bewunderung, aehnlich wie 'irre' oder 'verrueckt'. Das Ankerjahr ist eine repraesentative Belegstelle, keine Ursache.

triggers.label: emergence of funk music
triggers.description: The rise of funk as an African-American music genre (James Brown et al.) carried the word from 'bad smell' to a marker of style and soul.

triggers.label: Stonewall Riots and gay liberation movement
triggers.description: The 1969 Stonewall Riots and subsequent gay liberation movement accelerated the reappropriation of 'gay' as a neutral/positive self-identifier.

triggers.label: Colloquial bleaching of a cruelty word into an intensifier
triggers.description: No single datable event. 'Brutal' weakened in 20th-century colloquial speech from literal cruelty to a generic intensifier of harshness or unpleasantness (brutal weather, the brutal truth). The anchor year is a representative attestation of the intensifier use, not a cause.

triggers.label: Monty Python's Flying Circus 'Spam' sketch (1970)
triggers.description: The 1970 Monty Python sketch featuring 'Spam' repeated ad nauseam embedded the word's association with unwanted repetitive content in English geek culture, which internet users later applied to bulk email.

triggers.label: 1968 student protest movement
triggers.description: Emerged in West German usage around 1970/71, describing protest attitude of the 68er movement; word of the new protest generation

triggers.label: the modern environmental movement and the rise of Green politics
triggers.description: The environmental movement of the 1970s-80s (Earth Day 1970, Greenpeace 1971, and the founding of Green parties) attached an ecological-political meaning to 'green', making it shorthand for environmentally friendly.

triggers.label: Computer virus concept introduction and adoption
West German youth slang of the 1970s-80s
triggers.description: West German youth culture of the 1970s repurposed 'geil' as an intensifier of approval, analogous to English 'wicked' or 'sick'. The Veltins beer slogan 'Geil ist geil' (1990s) anchored the shift in mainstream usage.

triggers.label: RAF German Autumn terrorism 1977
triggers.description: Referred to underground networks related to terrorism during the 'German Autumn' (RAF terrorism era)

triggers.label: RAF Schleyer kidnapping 1977
triggers.description: Connected to RAF kidnapping of Hanns Martin Schleyer; safe-house concept entered public vocabulary

triggers.label: US TV series Holocaust broadcast in Germany
triggers.description: Established in German public discourse following the broadcast of the US TV series 'Holocaust – Die Geschichte der Familie Weiss'

triggers.label: West German anti-terrorism policing 1970s
triggers.description: Grid-search police method introduced due to 1970s terrorist threats; became publicly known investigative term

triggers.label: Youth-culture slang reversal of a negative term
triggers.description: No single datable event. Like 'bad', 'wicked' and 'ill', 'sick' was reversed to a term of approval in late 20th-century youth slang (skate, surf, hip hop), where extremity itself is praised. The anchor year is a representative decade, not a cause.

triggers.label: German Green Party founding and grassroots activism
NATO double-track decision nuclear debate
triggers.description: Related to NATO double-track decision debates on nuclear disarmament

triggers.label: CDU FDP government social welfare cuts 1982
triggers.description: Criticized the new CDU-FDP government for allegedly favoring egoism over social welfare; social critique term

triggers.label: NATO rearmament peace movement protests 1983
triggers.description: Described peace movement protests against NATO rearmament in autumn 1983

triggers.label: Apple Macintosh launch and personal computing revolution
triggers.description: The launch of the Apple Macintosh in 1984 and the subsequent spread of GUI-based personal computers brought the computer 'mouse' metaphor into mainstream German vocabulary.

triggers.label: naming of the computer virus and early PC infections
triggers.description: Fred Cohen defined and named the 'computer virus' in 1983-1984; the Brain virus (1986) and the spread of personal computers carried the term into German as 'Computervirus'.

triggers.label: catalytic converter environmental debate Germany
triggers.description: Discussed mandatory catalytic converter technology in automobiles amid environmental awareness

triggers.label: Austrian glycol wine scandal 1985
triggers.description: Resulted from the glycol wine scandal (Weinskandal) where Austrian wines were adulterated with diethylene glycol

triggers.label: Jugendsprachliche Aufwertung eines Negativworts
triggers.description: Kein einzelnes datierbares Ereignis. In der Jugendsprache seit den 1980er Jahren wurde 'krass' vom negativen 'extrem schlimm' zu einem allgemeinen, oft positiven Verstaerker. Das Ankerjahr ist eine repraesentative Belegstelle, keine Ursache.

triggers.label: Chernobyl nuclear disaster April 1986
triggers.description: Named after the Chernobyl nuclear reactor catastrophe on April 26, 1986

triggers.label: HIV AIDS epidemic public awareness campaign
triggers.description: Reflected growing public fears about the HIV/AIDS immunodeficiency disease and prevention

triggers.label: West German healthcare cost reform 1988
triggers.description: Addressed government attempts to limit medication costs through healthcare reform

triggers.label: Fall of the Berlin Wall November 1989
triggers.description: Marked the fall of the Berlin Wall and introduction of travel freedom for East Germans

triggers.label: Fall of the Berlin Wall (Mauerfall)
triggers.description: The opening of the Berlin Wall on 9 November 1989 and the subsequent collapse of the GDR gave 'die Wende' its specific historical referent.

triggers.label: Fall of Berlin Wall and Cold War cyberattacks
German reunification October 1990
triggers.description: Connected to German reunification in 1990; new term for former East German states

triggers.label: Queer Nation and ACT UP activist reappropriation
triggers.description: Queer Nation (founded 1990) and ACT UP activists deliberately reclaimed 'queer' as an empowering self-description, reversing its slur status.

triggers.label: rise of internet FTP mirror sites
triggers.description: As anonymous-FTP archives and software distribution grew across the early internet, popular archives were duplicated onto 'mirror' servers to share load and improve access, fixing the computing sense in the early 1990s.

triggers.label: Hoyerswerda anti-foreigner riots East Germany 1991
triggers.description: Xenophobic slogan used during Hoyerswerda riots; dehumanizing demand for ethnic cleansing of neighborhoods

triggers.label: post-reunification East-West German social tensions
triggers.description: Portmanteau of 'Besserwisser' and 'Wessi'; emerged after reunification describing condescending West Germans

triggers.label: Yugoslav Wars ethnic cleansing 1992
triggers.description: Propaganda formula used to euphemize genocide in the Yugoslav Wars

triggers.label: German party financing scandals public distrust 1992
triggers.description: Reflected citizens' growing dissatisfaction with politics amid scandals and party financing affairs

triggers.label: Usenet 'trolling for newbies' coinage (early 1990s)
triggers.description: On Usenet around 1992, 'trolling' (from the fishing sense of trailing bait) named the practice of posting provocative messages to bait responses; the agent noun 'troll' followed and broadened across the social web.

triggers.label: German austerity welfare state cuts 1993
triggers.description: Discussed broad reductions in state social services under austerity measures

triggers.label: German far-right anti-immigration rhetoric 1993
triggers.description: Spurious nativist argument against immigration; coded xenophobic language

triggers.label: Usenet bulk posting incidents (early 1990s)
triggers.description: Early Usenet flooding incidents (notably Canter and Siegel's 1994 bulk posting) were labelled 'spamming' by analogy with the Monty Python sketch, cementing the digital sense.

triggers.label: World Wide Web and Mosaic browser launch
triggers.description: The public launch of the WWW and graphical browsers (Mosaic 1993, Netscape 1994) brought the internet to a mass audience; German adopted 'das Netz' for the internet and the web, mirroring the English 'web' metaphor.

triggers.label: Hilmar Kopper Deutsche Bank peanuts statement 1994
triggers.description: Derogatory banking jargon; Deutsche Bank CEO Hilmar Kopper called 50 million DM debt of small creditors 'peanuts'

triggers.label: multiple simultaneous German elections 1994
triggers.description: Designated 1994 as a year with unusually many elections at federal, state, and European level

triggers.label: German parliamentary salary increase euphemism 1995
triggers.description: Euphemism masking parliamentary salary increases; politicians raising own salaries while cutting social benefits

triggers.label: early internet and digital media revolution 1995
triggers.description: Served as keyword for the journey into the new media world; rise of internet and digital media

triggers.label: German adaptation of English fashion terms
German pension system demographic ageing debate
triggers.description: Fear-inducing nature metaphor treating elderly population as a flood threatening the pension system

triggers.label: German federal austerity package 1996
triggers.description: Bundled cost-saving government measures during austerity consolidation politics

triggers.label: late Kohl government reform paralysis 1997
triggers.description: Criticized the lack of necessary political reforms; gridlock in German reform politics

triggers.label: German long-term unemployment stigma debate 1997
triggers.description: Dehumanizing description of long-term unemployed persons as 'prosperity trash'

triggers.label: first federal SPD Greens coalition 1998
triggers.description: Marked the first federal red-green coalition under Gerhard Schröder

triggers.label: German pension costs elderly demographic cynicism 1998
triggers.description: Cynical bureaucratic euphemism suggesting elderly should die early for social compatibility

triggers.label: NATO Kosovo bombing civilian casualties 1999
triggers.description: Military jargon minimizing killing of innocent civilians in NATO Kosovo bombing as incidental

triggers.label: Y2K millennium transition year 2000
triggers.description: Referenced the approaching year 2000 and Y2K fears and celebrations

triggers.label: rise of the World Wide Web and email forwarding culture
triggers.description: The 1990s web popularised the concept of content spreading exponentially like a biological virus. The term 'viral marketing' (Jurvetson/Draper 1997) anchored the metaphor.

triggers.label: German neo-Nazi far-right territorial extremism
triggers.description: Neo-Nazi euphemism describing areas terrorized by far-right extremists as 'nationally liberated zones'

triggers.label: CDU Kohl illegal party donations scandal
triggers.description: Exposed illegal CDU party donation practices from the 1990s; Kohl donation scandal

triggers.label: Eminem's song 'Stan' (2000)
triggers.description: Eminem's 2000 song 'Stan', about a fan whose devotion turns deadly, gave its name to the common noun for an obsessive fan; social-media fandom later generalised it into the verb 'to stan'.

triggers.label: September 11 terrorist attacks United States 2001
triggers.description: Named for the 9/11 US terror attacks which dominated public discourse globally

triggers.label: September 11 attacks Taliban al-Qaeda terrorism
triggers.description: Self-designation by Taliban and al-Qaeda terrorists glorifying religious violence after 9/11

triggers.label: Hartz labor reform individual self-employment model Germany
triggers.description: Hartz Commission term reducing individuals to economic market value; self-employment startup subsidy

triggers.label: Euro currency introduction price perception Germany
triggers.description: Described perceived price increases after Euro currency introduction; portmanteau of 'teuer' and 'Euro'

triggers.label: Rumsfeld old Europe statement Iraq War 2003
triggers.description: Referenced US Defense Secretary Donald Rumsfeld's dismissive statement about European countries opposing Iraq War

triggers.label: Hohmann antisemitic Tätervolk speech Bundestag 2003
triggers.description: Unacceptable collective guilt accusation against Jewish people; used by German politician Martin Hohmann

triggers.label: Hartz IV labor market welfare reform Germany 2004
triggers.description: Designated labor market reform commission proposals; became symbol of welfare state restructuring

triggers.label: OECD education policy human capital discourse Germany
triggers.description: Economic term degrading human beings to mere capital assets; spread into education policy discourse

triggers.label: mobile internet and international data usage
tuition fees in public higher education
Social media and digital platform emergence
Angela Merkel elected first female German chancellor
triggers.description: Celebrated Angela Merkel's election as Germany's first female chancellor

triggers.label: German corporate restructuring mass layoff profits 2005
triggers.description: Profits from mass layoffs disguised as efficiency gains; cynical labor market terminology

triggers.label: rise of broadband streaming media services
triggers.description: Broadband internet and services such as YouTube (2005), Netflix streaming (2007) and Spotify (2008) made consuming audio and video as a continuous online flow mainstream, fixing the verb 'to stream' in the media sense.

triggers.label: Amazon Web Services launch and popularisation of cloud computing (2006)
triggers.description: Amazon's launch of AWS EC2 and S3 in 2006, followed by Eric Schmidt's use of 'cloud computing' at the 2006 Search Engine Strategies conference, anchored the term in mainstream technology discourse.

triggers.label: 2006 FIFA World Cup public viewing Germany
triggers.description: Connected to public viewing culture during the 2006 FIFA World Cup in Germany

triggers.label: German asylum deportation policy coerced departure euphemism
triggers.description: Asylum seekers under deportation threat have no genuine choice; cynical official bureaucratic language

triggers.label: Launch of Twitter social media platform (2006)
triggers.description: Twitter launched in 2006 with a deliberate bird metaphor; users organically adopted 'tweet' for a post by 2007, which Twitter later formalised in its UI.

triggers.label: German childcare home care benefit political debate 2007
triggers.description: Pejorative term disparaging childcare benefit for stay-at-home parents as 'stove bonus'

triggers.label: IPCC Fourth Assessment Report climate crisis 2007
triggers.description: Addressed consequences of uncontrolled global warming amid IPCC reports and climate debate

triggers.label: Lehman Brothers collapse global financial crisis 2008
triggers.description: Named the global banking and financial crisis triggered by the collapse of Lehman Brothers

triggers.label: youth party culture over-30 parties generational slang
triggers.description: Youth slang for Ü30 parties (over-30 parties); portmanteau treating older party-goers as spoiled meat

triggers.label: 2008 global financial crisis bank bailout rhetoric
triggers.description: Reverses causality in financial crisis: banks described as 'suffering' despite causing the crisis

triggers.label: Facebook 'friend' / 'unfriend' interface (mid-2000s)
triggers.description: Facebook's friends model, opened to the general public in 2006, gave 'friend' and 'unfriend' precise platform meanings; 'unfriend' was named New Oxford American Dictionary Word of the Year in 2009.

triggers.label: Facebook Like button launch (2009)
triggers.description: Facebook launched its 'Like' button in February 2009; the feature spread across the web and social platforms, turning the everyday verb into a one-click social endorsement and a countable noun ('likes').

triggers.label: German economic stimulus cash for clunkers program 2009
triggers.description: Described cash-for-clunkers vehicle scrapping incentive program as economic stimulus

triggers.label: German employer anti-union works council rhetoric 2009
triggers.description: Trade union representative described workers' councils as 'contaminated with works council disease'

triggers.label: Hartz IV unemployment welfare stigma youth slang
triggers.description: Derived from Hartz IV welfare reform; means to be unemployed, hang around; Hartz IV as cultural stigma

triggers.label: Angela Merkel's repeated use of 'alternativlos' 2009-2010
triggers.description: Chancellor Merkel systematically used 'alternativlos' to justify the Euro rescue package, health reform, and Stuttgart 21, prompting the Gesellschaft fuer deutsche Sprache to name it Unwort des Jahres 2010.

triggers.label: Lil B's reappropriation of 'based' (c. 2010)
triggers.description: Around 2010 the rapper Lil B redefined 'based', previously a drug-related slur, to mean being positive and true to oneself; internet communities then spread it as a term of approval.

triggers.label: Merkel austerity policy alternativlos rhetoric 2010
triggers.description: Merkel's claim that austerity policy decisions had no alternatives; falsely forecloses political debate

triggers.label: WikiLeaks Julian Assange secret information leaks
triggers.description: Anonymous publication of secret information via internet; WikiLeaks made the term mainstream

triggers.label: youth party culture declining standards social commentary
triggers.description: Portmanteau of Niveau (level) and limbo dance; steady decline of standards, especially at parties

triggers.label: Stuttgart 21 railway protest movement Germany 2010
triggers.description: Reflected emerging angry citizen protest culture, coined during Stuttgart 21 railway protests

triggers.label: Spiegel essay 'Der Wutbuerger' by Dirk Kurbjuweit
triggers.description: The essay in Der Spiegel Issue 41 of 11 October 2010 coined the term and made it immediately prominent, tying it to Stuttgart 21 protests.

triggers.label: Social-media meme culture (2010s, r/cringe)
triggers.description: Merriam-Webster records the slang adjective from 1983. Online meme and reaction culture, including the r/cringe subreddit (founded 2011) and the 'cringe compilation' video genre, spread the predicative adjective/noun use widely in the 2010s but did not coin it.

triggers.label: Eli Pariser, 'The Filter Bubble' (2011)
triggers.description: Eli Pariser's 2011 book 'The Filter Bubble' coined the concept; the German loan-translation 'Filterblase' became the standard term for algorithmic information isolation in German media debate.

triggers.label: NSU neo-Nazi murder series Turkish German victims
triggers.description: Reduces NSU neo-Nazi hate crimes murdering Turkish-German business owners to food metaphor

triggers.label: social media outrage culture Twitter Facebook rise Germany
triggers.description: Wave of online outrage in social networks; entered German public and even official discourse

triggers.label: Fukushima nuclear disaster bank stress tests 2011
triggers.description: Referenced extensive testing of nuclear power plants and banks after Fukushima disaster

triggers.label: hip-hop culture swag concept German youth language adoption
triggers.description: Borrowed from hip-hop culture; enviously admired cool, stylish charisma and attitude

triggers.label: Established as mainstream German internet discourse term
Kickstarter internet crowdfunding startup culture rise
triggers.description: Raising capital from many small individual contributions via internet; new startup and creative funding model

triggers.label: German misogynist victim-blaming discourse sexual violence
triggers.description: Accuses women of holding a subscription to victimhood; trivializes sexual violence claims

triggers.label: European sovereign debt crisis bailout packages
triggers.description: Described regular government emergency financial rescue packages during Euro debt crisis

triggers.label: Drake YOLO hip-hop internet meme spread 2012
triggers.description: Acronym 'you only live once'; internet/Drake hip-hop phrase urging to seize opportunities

triggers.label: Black Lives Matter movement
triggers.description: The BLM movement, especially after 2013, mainstreamed 'woke' as a marker of social-justice awareness.

triggers.label: Haftbefehl Babo rap song German youth slang spread 2013
triggers.description: Turkish/Kurdish origin word for boss, leader; spread through German hip-hop especially Haftbefehl song

triggers.label: NSA surveillance scandal Germany Watergate suffix spread
triggers.description: Suffix for designating scandals; derived from Watergate; NSA-Gate and other scandals spread the suffix

triggers.label: CDU SPD grand coalition negotiations Germany 2013
triggers.description: Abbreviated 'Große Koalition'; CDU/CSU-SPD grand coalition negotiations after federal election

triggers.label: EU free movement benefit tourism debate Germany 2013
triggers.description: Stigmatizes Eastern European EU migrants as benefit tourists; contested by research showing opposite

triggers.label: racial sensitivity debate blackface Germany theater carnival
triggers.description: White actors painted black in theatrical/carnival contexts; racial sensitivity debate reached Germany

triggers.label: German internet meme ironic praise social media spread
triggers.description: Ironic internet meme phrase meaning 'things are going well for you'; spread through social media

triggers.label: 25th anniversary Berlin Wall fall light installation 2014
triggers.description: Referenced the light installation for the 25th anniversary of the Fall of the Berlin Wall

triggers.label: Pegida movement right-wing press delegitimization 2014
triggers.description: Nazi-era attack on press freedom revived by Pegida and right-wing movements; echoes Goebbels propaganda

triggers.label: PEGIDA movement demonstrations, Dresden 2014
triggers.description: From October 2014, PEGIDA demonstrators chanted 'Luegenpresse' at journalists, reactivating a Nazi-era slur and cementing its association with the far-right. Unwort des Jahres 2014.

triggers.label: German gender-inclusive language debate (2010s)
triggers.description: The 2010s German debate over gender-inclusive language popularised the asterisk form (Leser*innen); DWDS records the inclusive sense and a frequency rise across the decade (corpus examples 2009-2018).

triggers.label: European migration crisis Germany 2015
triggers.description: Contextualized mass migration of refugees from Middle East and Africa to Europe and Germany

triggers.label: refugee crisis Germany right-wing backlash humanitarian critics 2015
triggers.description: Ridicules refugee advocates as naive do-gooders; used to undermine compassion during refugee crisis

triggers.label: European refugee crisis Germany welcome culture 2015
triggers.description: English slogan for welcome culture during refugee crisis; used in German protests and media

triggers.label: smartphone addiction distracted pedestrian culture youth
triggers.description: Portmanteau of smartphone and zombie; people distracted by devices; spread through mobile phone culture

triggers.label: Mainstreaming of mobile dating apps (Tinder, 2012-)
triggers.description: Swipe-based mobile dating apps such as Tinder (launched 2012) normalised low-friction matching and easy disappearance, and the term 'ghosting' for abrupt no-explanation withdrawal spread rapidly in 2015.

triggers.label: Gutmensch chosen as Unwort des Jahres 2015
triggers.description: In January 2016 a language-critical jury named Gutmensch the non-word of 2015, reflecting how the term had become a right-wing polemical weapon to dismiss liberal opponents.

triggers.label: Rise of Instagram and influencer marketing (mid-2010s)
triggers.description: The growth of Instagram (launched 2010) and the surrounding creator economy turned 'influencer' into a job title for paid social-media promoters, narrowing the generic sense to this commercial meaning by the mid-2010s.

triggers.label: Political and media discourse surge (2016-2018)
triggers.description: From the 2016 US election onward, commentators applied 'gaslighting' to political messaging and misinformation, broadening it from a clinical term to a general label for deceptive manipulation; Merriam-Webster reported a steep lookup and usage rise.

triggers.label: Trump election Brexit deliberate disinformation fake news 2016
triggers.description: Deliberately false information spread as news; Trump election and Brexit made term ubiquitous

triggers.label: hip-hop fly aesthetic German youth language adoption
triggers.description: From hip-hop culture meaning to be attractive, stylish, great; someone who stands out positively

triggers.label: Brexit Trump post-truth politics rise 2016
